Glossary About
Medieval Latin Scholasticism

Gregory of Rimini

c. 1300 – 1358

Divine illumination, will, and late scholastic theories of cognition.

Gregory of Rimini did not write a modern “philosophy of mind” treatise. The reconstruction has to be extracted mainly from his Lectura on the first two books of Peter Lombard’s Sentences, especially from questions on the soul, cognition, theological science, divine foreknowledge, future contingents, sin, and the cognition of angels. That is not a defect in the project: late-medieval Sentences commentaries were one of the major vehicles through which philosophical psychology was developed inside theology, alongside the better-known Aristotelian De anima commentary tradition. A recent Brepols volume on philosophical psychology in late-medieval Sentences commentaries explicitly lists topics such as the faculties of the soul, intuitive and abstractive cognition, the immortality of the soul, divine foreknowledge, and the knowability of species as psychological issues discussed in this theological genre.

The central thesis of this report is that Gregory’s philosophy of mind is best understood as an act-centered, judgment-centered, graded theory of cognition. Human cognition begins from singular things and sensory causation, but it reaches full knowledge only in complex acts of judgment. The object of knowledge is not simply a thing, nor simply a sentence, but the total signifiable content of a proposition, the famous complexe significabile. Human thought is embodied and individual, but mental content is not reducible to bodily stimulation. Animals have sensory judgments without full intellect. Angels are finite immaterial minds whose knowledge still depends on things and develops over time. God’s mind is not a theological ornament added to the theory; it is the limiting case that clarifies truth, modality, direct cognition, and the relation between knowing and being.

1. Intellectual setting and why Gregory matters for philosophy of mind

Gregory of Rimini, an Augustinian Hermit born around 1300 and dead in 1358, studied and taught in a world shaped by Parisian and Oxford debates after Peter Auriol, Ockham, Walter Chatton, and Adam Wodeham. His main philosophical-theological work is his commentary on the first two books of the Sentences, critically edited in the late twentieth century. The Stanford Encyclopedia presents him as the first major thinker to integrate post-Ockham Oxford discussion with post-Auriol Parisian discussion, and emphasizes the long influence of his work in later scholastic and Reformation debates.

The older labels “nominalist,” “realist,” and “conceptualist” are especially dangerous here. French and Italian scholarship both stress that Gregory’s reception has oscillated: he was once treated as a major representative of the “nominales,” then Hubert Élie’s work on the complexe significabile encouraged a more realist reading, while Gilson famously wondered whether he was an Ockhamist sheltered behind Augustine or an Augustinian accommodating Ockhamist conclusions. Treccani’s Italian entry warns that these taxonomies are historically dated and systematically misleading unless grounded in close textual analysis.

For philosophy of mind, Gregory is not merely a derivative compiler. His importance lies in the way he joins metaphysics of the soul, semantics, epistemology, modal logic, theology, and moral psychology. The result is not a modern “mind-body problem” in Cartesian terms, but a rich medieval theory of mental acts, intentionality, judgment, representation, privacy, other minds, animal cognition, angelic cognition, and divine knowledge.

2. The human soul: individualized, hylomorphic, and anti-Averroist

Gregory’s basic anthropology is hylomorphic. The human soul is the substantial form of a living body, and the rational soul is the single substantial form of the human being. In this respect he resists any account on which the intellect is merely an external cognitive principle added to human beings at the moment of knowing. Against the Averroist thesis of a single possible intellect for the whole human species, Gregory defends the multiplication of possible intellects in individual human beings. Treccani summarizes his argument as combining Augustinian introspective psychology with an Ockhamist principle of identity: if one possible intellect were strictly identical for all humans, then what one person thinks would be thought by every other; but internal experience shows that one person does not simply share another’s thoughts by a mental process.

This is philosophically important. Gregory’s anti-Averroism is not only a doctrine about Aristotle’s De anima; it is a thesis about mental individuation and privacy. Thoughts are acts of an individual soul, not episodes in a shared species-intellect. The privacy of mental acts becomes one of the deep commitments of his psychology: other minds are real, but their acts are not simply available to me as my own are.

Gregory also denies a real distinction between the human soul and its faculties. Against Thomas Aquinas on this point, he holds that the soul is the direct principle even of sensation and that there is no need to posit a sensory power as a distinct quality inhering in the soul. He also rejects a distinct “form of corporeity” in addition to the rational soul. The human being is therefore not a stack of separately real psychic modules, but a unity whose rational soul is the principle of both intellectual and sensory operations.

The result is a striking model: Gregory is not a materialist, because the soul is a genuine substantial form and the principle of intellectual acts; he is not a Cartesian dualist, because the soul is the form of the living body; he is not an Averroist, because there is no shared species-intellect; and he is not a strong faculty-realist, because faculties are not really distinct parts or qualities over and above the soul. His philosophy of mind is therefore a unitary hylomorphic psychology: one individual human soul performs diverse cognitive and appetitive acts under different causal conditions.

3. The architecture of human cognition: simple, complex, sensory, intellectual, direct, mediated

Gregory’s cognitive theory is organized around several distinctions. Treccani identifies three especially important pairs: simple versus complex cognition, sensory versus intellectual cognition, and direct/immediate versus indirect/mediated cognition. Simple cognition is the apprehension of an atomic object or item; complex cognition is propositional and affirmative or negative, describing the metaphysical structure of an item or relations among items. Truth is fully and adequately grasped only in complex cognition, where the intellect combines or separates what simple cognition initially presents.

This makes Gregory’s theory judgment-centered. Simple apprehension is not negligible, but it is not yet full knowledge. The mind does not merely register objects; it articulates what is the case. Knowledge reaches completion when the intellect says, in effect, that something is or is not thus. That is why Gregory’s philosophy of mind cannot be separated from his logic and semantics.

The sensory/intellectual distinction is subtler than a simple opposition between body and mind. In Gregory’s account of human cognition, sensory and intellectual cognition are not rivals, because every human cognition requires the intellect; sensory cognition adds the causal contribution of a sense organ and a sensible object. This means that human sensory cognition is already integrated into the operation of the soul as cognitive principle, even though it depends causally on embodied sensory organs.

Gregory also distinguishes direct and mediated cognition. Direct cognition is immediately of a really existing entity. Mediated cognition is immediately of a mental species or image and only through that species of the real object represented. Treccani notes that Gregory distinguishes concrete sensory images from abstract images or conceptus ficti, which the intellect forms to serve as reminders or general representations for groups of similar sensible objects.

The Stanford Encyclopedia describes the same terrain using the distinction between intuitive and abstractive cognition. Gregory’s version is unusual because he distinguishes these cognitions by their objects rather than by two fundamentally different mental modes. Intuitive cognition terminates immediately in an extramental object; abstractive cognition terminates in the object’s species in the soul. Species are important for him: universal concepts are qualities really distinct from cognitive acts, and they remain in the soul.

This produces a moderated empiricism. Universals are not directly encountered as extramental universals. They are formed by the soul after prior apprehensions of singulars, and sensory experience plays a major role in intellectual cognition. Yet Gregory is not a simple nominalist eliminativist about mental representation, because species and universal concepts have real psychological status as qualities or images in the soul.

The theory also has room for error. Gregory allows that an intuitive cognition can concern a non-existing object when caused by the species of a present object, as in cases of deceptive appearance. That matters for philosophy of mind because it shows that intentionality is not identical with veridical contact: a cognitive act can be directed as though toward something that is not in fact there in the way it is represented.

4. From apprehension to judgment: the mind as discursive articulator of reality

For Gregory, the apprehension of a substantial essence itself tends toward judgment. Treccani emphasizes that, because of the discursive constitution of our intellect, we grasp as discursively articulated what empirical reality presents as unified. The intellect reconstructs the metaphysical structure of an object through acts that can be expressed propositionally. Thus, “simple” cognition is not an isolated mental atom; complex cognition is rooted inside it as its completion and perfection.

This is one of Gregory’s most important contributions to a philosophy of mind. Human understanding is not merely receptivity to things. It is an operation that articulates things. When we know Socrates, we do not merely encounter an individual; we judge that Socrates is human, that he has such-and-such properties, and that certain relations hold. The mind’s proper achievement is not bare acquaintance but structured cognition.

That explains why the object of knowledge cannot be, for Gregory, merely the individual substance as such. Nor can it simply be the essence in abstraction. The adequate object of human knowledge is the total meaning of the proposition: what is signified when the proposition says something complex about reality. Treccani identifies this as the central problem of Gregory’s epistemology and as the part of his system most intensely studied by later scholarship.

5. The complexe significabile: object of knowledge, belief, and science

Gregory inherits the problem of the object of scientific knowledge from Ockham, Chatton, and Wodeham. Ockham had taken the object of science to be the conclusion of a syllogism; Chatton had pushed toward an extramental res; Wodeham developed the doctrine of the complexe significabile. Gregory rejects Ockham’s view because strict science requires something necessary and stable rather than a contingent mental or linguistic conclusion. He also rejects a simple identification with created things, since created beings are contingent whereas scientific knowledge requires an object of necessity.

The complexe significabile is what a proposition signifies in a complex way. It is not simply the proposition itself, not merely the individual things named by the proposition, and not straightforwardly an ordinary entity in the world. The Stanford Encyclopedia notes that its status remains debated: some readings treat it as a kind of “nothing,” some as mind-independent but not an entity, and some as mind-dependent.

The Italian Treccani interpretation gives Gregory a more robust ontological ambition: he wants a real correlate of propositional signification, something unitary corresponding to the whole proposition rather than just its subject and predicate, but not a categorical entity like a substance or accident. Treccani also notes the difficulty this creates: traditional ontology lacks an easy slot for such objects. The complexe significabile is real in the sense of being the object of possible signification, but it is not a “thing” in the ordinary categorial sense.

For philosophy of mind, this is decisive. Gregory’s theory separates at least four items that are easily confused: the mental act, the linguistic sentence, the individual things spoken of, and the complex signifiable content known or believed. A belief is not merely about a sentence; nor is it merely about a bare object. It is about the total complex content that the sentence or mental act signifies.

This makes Gregory a medieval theorist of intentional content in a strong sense. The mind is directed not only toward things but toward what is signifiable as being the case. However, one should resist the temptation to identify the complexe significabile too quickly with a modern Fregean proposition or a contemporary fact. Gregory’s object is proposition-like, fact-like, and metaphysically unusual, but it belongs to a theological-logical ontology in which truth is also ultimately related to God as first truth.

6. Mental propositions, assent, and the unity of mental acts

Gregory’s account of mental propositions is one of the places where his philosophy of mind becomes especially subtle. He differs from Wodeham by distinguishing two kinds of mental propositions. The Stanford Encyclopedia reports that, for non-linguistic mental propositions, formation and assent cannot be dissociated: assent is itself a kind of mental utterance.

Costantino Marmo’s Italian study gives a useful interpretation of this point. Gregory distinguishes one kind of mental enunciation modeled on spoken language from a deeper kind not made in the likeness of any spoken language. This deeper kind corresponds to Augustine’s “words of no language” and functions as a natural sign. Only this second kind can be at once proposition, assent, and knowledge.

The implication is that Gregory does not simply imagine thought as silent speech. Some mental propositions are not internal sentences composed in the same way spoken sentences are composed. Marmo emphasizes that Gregory’s mental propositions are not strictly built out of subject and predicate concepts as lower-level parts; their complexity lies in their equivalence of signification to many spoken or written expressions.

This is philosophically rich but also tense. On one side, Gregory’s theory of judgment uses the familiar propositional structure of subject, copula, and predicate. On the other side, his deep mental proposition can be a single mental act whose semantic complexity is not mirrored by psychological compositionality. The mind may therefore have semantically structured content without that content being assembled from mental words in the way a spoken sentence is assembled from vocal signs.

A modern reconstruction would say: Gregory separates semantic complexity from psychological compositionality. A thought can signify complexly even if the act of thinking is psychologically one. That claim makes him surprisingly relevant to debates about whether propositional thought requires an internal language with syntactic parts.

7. Mental privacy and the problem of other minds

Gregory’s anti-Averroist argument already implies that my mental acts are not directly shared with all other human beings. His broader theory of inner language reinforces this. In Marmo’s account of Gregory’s angelology and semiotics, the innermost level of thought is accessible only to the thinker and to God; other minds require signs or communicative acts to know it.

This yields a real medieval problem of other minds. Gregory does not doubt that other humans and animals have mental acts, but he treats their inner states as something to be inferred from signs, behavior, and communicative manifestation. The privacy of mind is not skepticism-inducing in a modern Cartesian way, but it is structurally present: first-person mental access and third-person attribution are different kinds of access.

The same theme appears in his treatment of animals. According to Anselm Oelze’s study, Gregory accepts that we do not have immediate access to animal mental states; we infer them probabilistically from behavior. This is not behaviorism, because the inference is to real internal judgments, but it is also not naive projection, because Gregory explicitly acknowledges the epistemic gap.

8. Animal minds: sensory judgment without full rational intellect

Gregory’s discussion of animals is one of the most valuable parts of his potential philosophy of mind. He rejects a simple stimulus-response account of animal behavior. Oelze notes Gregory’s example of a dog and bread: the same sensory stimulus can produce different responses depending on the animal’s condition, so one must suppose that the animal makes a sensory judgment about whether to eat.

Yet Gregory does not collapse animal cognition into human rational cognition. He does not treat animal judgment as intellectual or propositional in the full human sense. Oelze characterizes it as a non-propositional sensory judgment, a lower-level judgment restricted to particulars, sensible objects, and practically relevant situations. Animals do not grasp universals or insensible objects in the way human intellect does.

Gregory also uses the traditional “dialectical dog” case, where a hunting dog at a crossroads appears to reason disjunctively. He does not conclude that the dog performs a syllogism. Rather, the dog judges “as if reasoning”: it has complex cognition, but not a formal inferential process. Oelze’s account stresses that Gregory, like Aquinas and Scotus, denies genuine syllogistic reasoning to nonhuman animals while still granting them complex sensory cognition.

This is a sophisticated graded theory of mind. Gregory’s animals are not machines; they have intentional, error-prone, behavior-guiding sensory judgments. But they are not rational humans in miniature; they lack universal, discursive, intellectual cognition. His position therefore sits between crude anthropomorphism and crude mechanistic reduction.

For a systematic philosophy of mind, this gives Gregory a hierarchy of cognitive powers: sensory discrimination, sensory judgment, intellectual apprehension, propositional judgment, scientific knowledge, angelic intellection, and divine knowledge. “Mind” is not univocal across all creatures, but neither is it restricted to adult human propositional reasoning.

9. Emotions, will, and practical reason

Gregory’s philosophy of mind also includes an account of affective life. Dominik Perler’s study of fourteenth-century debates on the passions of the soul shows that Gregory rejects Adam Wodeham’s tendency to treat passions of the will as involving additional higher-level cognitions. Gregory criticizes such multiplication of cognitions as unnecessary. On his view, the intellect already provides the descriptive and evaluative cognition; the will contributes the affective component, such as love or hate, but does not produce a further cognition of its own.

This gives Gregory a parsimonious theory of emotion. Emotions are not blind bodily pushes, because they depend on cognition and evaluation. But the will does not become a second intellect. The affective dimension is real, yet it is not an additional act of knowing. In modern terms, Gregory separates the representational-evaluative component of emotion from the affective-volitional component while insisting that both belong to the full psychology of passion.

His moral psychology is also rationalist in a strong sense. Orazio Grassi’s Italian study of Gregory’s si Deus ipse non esset argument shows that Gregory defines sin as voluntary action against right reason and treats natural law as equivalent to the right judgment of reason. Gregory’s account of moral wrong is therefore not mere disobedience to an arbitrary command; it is a failure of voluntary action to conform to recta ratio.

Grassi further emphasizes that Gregory’s practical reason has first principles analogous to the first principles of speculative sciences. These principles are immediately evident and true for practical reason in a way comparable to mathematical principles for theoretical reason. On this interpretation, Gregory’s moral doctrine is anti-voluntarist: God too cannot act against right reason, cannot command actions intrinsically contrary to right reason, and cannot be made into a source of irrational moral arbitrariness.

The philosophy-of-mind upshot is significant. Human agency involves intellect, will, affect, and practical reason, but these are not isolated modules. The intellect presents what is to be done; the will voluntarily loves, hates, chooses, or refuses; affective states add motivational and experiential force; and moral responsibility depends on the relation between voluntary action and right reason.

10. Angelic minds: finite immaterial cognition, not theological distraction

Gregory’s angelology is philosophically central because it tests what cognition would be like without human sensory organs. Clarisse Reynard’s 2024 French study emphasizes that angelic cognition is not simply a marginal theological curiosity: it exposes differences between human and angelic cognition and illuminates the status of intellect, species, embodiment, and object-dependence. She also notes that Gregory’s angelic cognition has been much less studied than his human epistemology.

Gregory’s main opponent here is Thomas Aquinas, especially on the question of concreated species. Aquinas holds that angels have innate species by which they know corporeal things. Gregory rejects this. Reynard shows that Gregory’s critique focuses on two points: knowledge of corporeal singulars cannot be independent of those singulars, and the Thomistic view makes it difficult to explain how angels acquire new species.

For Gregory, an angel knows a material singular through a cognition caused at least partly by that singular itself. A species caused merely by God would not explain knowledge of this singular as this singular. Angelic cognition of corporeal singulars is therefore object-dependent. Species are relevant when the object is absent; they arise from a prior intuitive act rather than being innately concreated as a complete stock of representations.

This is a major philosophical move. Gregory does not make angels into quasi-divine minds with complete innate knowledge. They are finite knowers whose cognition progresses as their intellections proceed. Reynard concludes that Gregory brings angelic cognition closer to human cognition than Thomas does: angels lack human sensory organs, but they still acquire cognitive resources and remain linked to things in the world.

Gregory’s angelology also deepens his theory of mental communication. Marmo argues that medieval discussions of angelic language functioned as thought experiments about thought, language, and mental representation, not merely as speculative theology. In Gregory’s account, the innermost mental level is accessible only to the thinker and to God; angels communicate by intellectual signs or spiritual discourses directed to another angel. Other angels may access only this communicative level, not the deepest inner act.

The philosophical lesson is clear: immateriality does not entail omniscience, publicity, or innate completeness. A mind without bodily organs can still be finite, object-dependent, temporally progressive, and communicatively mediated. Angelic minds therefore occupy a middle place between human embodied cognition and divine direct cognition.

11. The mind of God: truth, modality, and direct cognition

Gregory’s treatment of God’s mind is indispensable for his philosophy of mind, especially because his semantics of truth and his theory of future contingents depend on divine knowledge. Treccani reports that Gregory distinguishes God’s knowledge of res from God’s knowledge of propositional meanings or enuntiabilia. God knows things directly, without an intermediary, and not merely through the divine essence as a mediating object in the way Peter Auriol suggested. God’s knowledge includes both simple apprehension or scientia visionis of things and judgment-like knowledge of truth and falsity with respect to propositional contents.

This matters for the complexe significabile. Treccani states that the truth or falsity of complex signifiables is finally explained through their relation to God as first and uncreated truth. Human propositions are true or false according to the truth or falsity of the complexe significabile they signify, and the complexe significabile itself is situated in relation to divine truth.

Gregory’s divine mind is therefore not just a bigger human mind. Human and angelic minds require acts, species, causal dependence, discursive articulation, and progression. God’s mind has direct, non-mediated knowledge of creatures and propositional truth. But Gregory’s account does not abandon logic when speaking of God. On the contrary, divine knowledge is one reason he insists on rigorous logic about truth, modality, and future contingents.

This is clearest in the problem of future contingents. Gregory rejects Peter Auriol’s suspension of bivalence for future contingent propositions. He holds that the Principle of Bivalence applies universally: future contingent propositions are already true or false. Yet their truth does not make future events absolutely necessary. True future contingent propositions are immutable and determinate, but only with necessity ex suppositione, not absolute necessity.

The underlying philosophy of mind is this: divine foreknowledge is not ignorance, but neither is truth a crude deterministic force. God’s knowing that something will happen does not make it absolutely necessary in the same way that a geometrical theorem is necessary. Gregory uses modal distinctions to preserve both divine knowledge and creaturely contingency.

Gregory’s account of divine rationality also belongs here. Treccani notes that he refuses both the claim that God could lie and the claim that divine power should be limited in a way that undermines divine prerogative. Grassi’s study of right reason adds that Gregory’s moral theory presents God as incapable of acting against right reason, not as an arbitrary will outside rational order.

There is a real scholarly dispute about how Gregory handles God and the past. The Stanford Encyclopedia says he probably thinks God cannot change the past; Treccani suggests that he seems to leave open the possibility in some way. That disagreement should not be flattened. It shows how difficult Gregory’s combination of divine power, logical consistency, and temporal modality remains.

12. A reconstructed systematic philosophy of mind

Taken together, Gregory’s scattered discussions yield a surprisingly comprehensive philosophy of mind.

First, mind is individuated by the individual soul. There is no shared possible intellect for all humans; mental acts belong to individual human beings. This grounds mental privacy and makes interpersonal cognition inferential rather than immediately shared.

Second, the human mind is hylomorphic rather than Cartesian. The rational soul is the form of the body and the direct principle of sensation and intellection. Gregory therefore avoids both materialist reduction and a dualism in which the mind is merely an external thinking substance attached to a body.

Third, cognition is act-centered. What matters are cognitive acts: apprehending, judging, assenting, knowing, loving, hating, and willing. Gregory is parsimonious about such acts. He resists multiplying cognitions when one act can do the work, as in his critique of Wodeham on passions and in his account of assent as a mental utterance.

Fourth, cognition is object-dependent. Human cognition begins from singulars and sensory causation; angelic cognition of corporeal singulars also depends at least partly on those singulars; even abstractive cognition depends on species generated from prior acts. Gregory is not an innatist about creaturely cognition in the strong Thomistic angelic sense.

Fifth, representation is real but not ultimate. Species, images, and concepts matter, especially in abstractive cognition and memory-like representation. Yet intuitive cognition aims at the extramental thing itself, and species are not a substitute for the world. Gregory’s representationalism is therefore not a retreat into internal mental objects.

Sixth, full knowledge is judgmental. Simple apprehension supplies terms or objects, but truth is achieved through complex cognition. The mind knows most fully when it articulates what is the case. This is why Gregory’s philosophy of mind is inseparable from his semantics of propositions.

Seventh, the object of knowledge is complex signifiable content. Gregory’s complexe significabile gives him a theory of intentional content that is neither merely mental nor merely linguistic nor merely identical with ordinary things. This is probably his most original contribution to philosophy of mind, because it clarifies what beliefs, judgments, and scientific acts are about.

Eighth, mental language is layered. Some mental propositions are like internalized linguistic structures, but the deepest mental proposition is a natural sign, not a sentence of any spoken language. This lets Gregory distinguish thought from speech while also explaining how thought can be expressed.

Ninth, cognition is graded across beings. Animals have sensory judgments without full intellectual reasoning. Humans have embodied sensory-intellectual judgment. Angels have finite immaterial cognition that is still object-dependent and progressive. God has direct, non-mediated knowledge of things and propositional truth. Gregory’s philosophy of mind is thus a hierarchy of cognitive forms, not a single model projected onto all beings.

Tenth, practical mind is rational and affective. The intellect supplies cognition and evaluation; the will adds affective-volitional orientation; right reason governs moral action. Gregory’s moral psychology is therefore neither pure intellectualism nor pure voluntarism.

13. Main tensions and unresolved questions

The first major tension is the ontology of the complexe significabile. Gregory needs it to be stable enough to serve as the object of knowledge and truth, but not a normal categorial entity. Later interpreters disagree about whether it is a kind of non-entity, a mind-independent non-entity, or a mind-dependent object. This ambiguity is not accidental; it is the price of trying to give propositional content a metaphysical role in a framework not originally built for facts or propositions in the modern sense.

The second tension concerns compositionality. Gregory’s theory of judgment often sounds subject-predicate-copula based, but his deeper mental propositions can be single acts whose complexity is not psychologically compositional. That is a fruitful tension, not merely an inconsistency: it forces us to distinguish the logical analysis of what is signified from the psychological structure of the act signifying it.

The third tension concerns sensory cognition. In human cognition, Gregory says sensory and intellectual cognition are integrated and that every human cognition involves intellect. But he also grants animals sensory judgments without full intellectual cognition. The best reconstruction is that Gregory treats human sensory cognition as intellect-involving because it belongs to the rational human soul, while animal sensory judgment is a lower-level cognitive act that can be complex without being intellectual in the strict human sense.

The fourth tension concerns divine knowledge and contingency. Gregory wants universal bivalence and robust divine foreknowledge, but he also wants future contingents to remain genuinely contingent. His distinction between absolute necessity and necessity ex suppositione is the technical solution, but the metaphysical pressure remains intense, especially in relation to predestination and the fixity of the past.

The fifth tension concerns theology and philosophy. Gregory cannot be read as doing “pure” psychology in isolation from God, angels, sin, grace, and theological science. But neither should his theology be treated as a non-philosophical overlay. In his work, divine and angelic cognition function as boundary cases for theories of knowledge, representation, communication, truth, and modality.

14. Concise final assessment

Gregory of Rimini’s philosophy of mind can be described as individualist, hylomorphic, representational, judgment-centered, semantically realist in tendency, psychologically parsimonious, and hierarchically graded.

Its center is not consciousness in the modern phenomenal sense, but intentional cognitive act. The mind is known through what it does: apprehending singulars, forming species, abstracting concepts, judging complex contents, assenting, knowing, loving, willing, and communicating. Its most original move is to insist that the proper object of scientific and judgmental cognition is the complex signifiable content of propositions. Its most expansive move is to treat animal, human, angelic, and divine minds as ordered but genuinely different cases of cognition. On that reading, Gregory offers not a fragmentary theological psychology, but one of the most ambitious late-medieval frameworks for understanding mind, meaning, judgment, and truth.

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
A personal research project by Fabián. · About · Glossary
